  • Hide All Code

Predicting Voter Behavior Using Multinomial Logistic Regression

A Multinomial Logistic Regression Analysis

Analysis Report

2026-04-26

1 Introduction

This report presents a Multinomial Logistic Regression model to predict voter category (always, sporadic, or rarely/never) using demographic and political data from a 2020 Ipsos survey conducted for FiveThirtyEight’s article Why Many Americans Don’t Vote.

Response variable: voter_category, a three-level factor:

  • rarely/never: voted in 0 or 1 eligible elections (baseline)
  • sporadic: voted in at least two, but fewer than all-but-one eligible elections
  • always: voted in all or all-but-one eligible elections

Predictors: Age (ppage), education (educ), race (race), gender (gender), household income (income_cat), and party identification (party, derived from Q30).

Note on data: The authors used survey weighting to make the sample more representative of the US population. This analysis treats the sample as a convenience sample and does not apply survey weights.

2 Setup

library(tidyverse)
library(tidymodels)
library(GGally)
library(ggformula)
library(broom)
library(knitr)
nonvoters <- read.csv("nonvoters.csv") %>%
  select(ppage, educ, race, gender, income_cat, Q30, voter_category)

glimpse(nonvoters)
## Rows: 5,836
## Columns: 7
## $ ppage          <int> 73, 90, 53, 58, 81, 61, 80, 68, 70, 83, 43, 42, 48, 52,…
## $ educ           <chr> "College", "College", "College", "Some college", "High …
## $ race           <chr> "White", "White", "White", "Black", "White", "White", "…
## $ gender         <chr> "Female", "Female", "Male", "Female", "Male", "Female",…
## $ income_cat     <chr> "$75-125k", "$125k or more", "$125k or more", "$40-75k"…
## $ Q30            <int> 2, 3, 2, 2, 1, 5, 1, 2, 1, 3, 1, 2, 1, 5, 1, 1, 2, 1, 5…
## $ voter_category <chr> "always", "always", "sporadic", "sporadic", "always", "…
cat("Rows:", nrow(nonvoters), "\n")
## Rows: 5836
cat("Columns:", ncol(nonvoters), "\n")
## Columns: 7

3 Data Preparation

3.1 Creating the party Variable and Setting Factor Levels

The raw Q30 variable encodes party identification numerically. We recode it into a readable factor with four levels, and set voter_category as an ordered factor with "rarely/never" as the baseline.

nonvoters <- nonvoters %>%
  mutate(
    # Recode Q30 into readable party labels
    party = case_when(
      Q30 == 1 ~ "Republican",
      Q30 == 2 ~ "Democrat",
      Q30 == 3 ~ "Independent",
      Q30 %in% c(4, 5, -1) ~ "Other"
    ),
    party = factor(party,
      levels = c("Other", "Independent", "Democrat", "Republican")),

    # Set voter_category factor with rarely/never as baseline
    voter_category = factor(voter_category,
      levels = c("rarely/never", "sporadic", "always"))
  )

# Verify factor levels
levels(nonvoters$voter_category)
## [1] "rarely/never" "sporadic"     "always"
levels(nonvoters$party)
## [1] "Other"       "Independent" "Democrat"    "Republican"

3.2 Distribution of Response Variable

nonvoters %>%
  count(voter_category) %>%
  mutate(pct = round(n / sum(n) * 100, 1)) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    caption = "Distribution of Voter Category",
    col.names = c("Voter Category", "Count", "Percentage (%)")
  )
Distribution of Voter Category
Voter Category Count Percentage (%)
rarely/never 1451 24.9
sporadic 2574 44.1
always 1811 31.0 
ggplot(nonvoters, aes(x = voter_category, fill = voter_category)) +
  geom_bar(show.legend = FALSE, width = 0.6) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "rarely/never" = "#F44336",
    "sporadic"     = "#FF9800",
    "always"       = "#4CAF50"
  )) +
  labs(
    title    = "Distribution of Self-Reported Voter Category",
    subtitle = "Sporadic voters make up the largest group",
    x = "Voter Category", y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 11)

4 Exploratory Data Analysis

4.1 Voter Category by Party Identification

nonvoters %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = party, fill = voter_category)) +
  geom_bar(position = "fill") +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "rarely/never" = "#F44336",
    "sporadic"     = "#FF9800",
    "always"       = "#4CAF50"
  )) +
  labs(
    title    = "Voter Category by Party Identification",
    subtitle = "Democrats and Republicans are more likely to be consistent voters",
    x = "Party Identification",
    y = "Proportion",
    fill = "Voter Category"
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 11)

Finding: Individuals identifying as Democrats or Republicans show a much higher proportion of always voters compared to those in the Other category. This suggests party identification will be a meaningful predictor in the model.

4.2 Voter Category by Race and Education

p_race <- nonvoters %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = race, fill = voter_category)) +
  geom_bar(position = "fill") +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "rarely/never" = "#F44336",
    "sporadic"     = "#FF9800",
    "always"       = "#4CAF50"
  )) +
  labs(title = "Voter Category by Race",
       x = NULL, y = "Proportion", fill = "Voter Category") +
  theme_bw(base_size = 10)

p_educ <- nonvoters %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = educ, fill = voter_category)) +
  geom_bar(position = "fill") +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "rarely/never" = "#F44336",
    "sporadic"     = "#FF9800",
    "always"       = "#4CAF50"
  )) +
  labs(title = "Voter Category by Education",
       x = NULL, y = "Proportion", fill = "Voter Category") +
  theme_bw(base_size = 10)

gridExtra::grid.arrange(p_race, p_educ, ncol = 2)

Finding: College-educated respondents are substantially more likely to be always voters. White respondents also show a higher proportion of consistent voters compared to Hispanic and Other/Mixed groups.

4.3 Voter Category by Age and Income

p_age <- nonvoters %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = voter_category, y = ppage, fill = voter_category)) +
  geom_boxplot(show.legend = FALSE, alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "rarely/never" = "#F44336",
    "sporadic"     = "#FF9800",
    "always"       = "#4CAF50"
  )) +
  labs(title = "Age by Voter Category",
       x = "Voter Category", y = "Age") +
  theme_bw(base_size = 10)

p_income <- nonvoters %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = income_cat, fill = voter_category)) +
  geom_bar(position = "fill") +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "rarely/never" = "#F44336",
    "sporadic"     = "#FF9800",
    "always"       = "#4CAF50"
  )) +
  labs(title = "Voter Category by Income",
       x = NULL, y = "Proportion", fill = "Voter Category") +
  theme_bw(base_size = 10)

gridExtra::grid.arrange(p_age, p_income, ncol = 2)

Finding: Always voters tend to be older on average. Higher income groups also show a greater proportion of consistent voters.

5 Model Specification

5.1 Why Multinomial Logistic Regression?

The response variable voter_category has three unordered classes. Standard binary logistic regression is not applicable here. Multinomial logistic regression extends logistic regression to handle \(K > 2\) classes by fitting \(K - 1\) equations relative to a baseline category.

With "rarely/never" as the baseline (\(k = 1\)), the model fits two log-odds equations:

\[\log\left(\frac{\hat{p}_{\text{sporadic}}}{\hat{p}_{\text{rarely/never}}}\right) = \hat\beta_{0,S} + \hat\beta_{1,S}x_1 + \cdots + \hat\beta_{p,S}x_p\]

\[\log\left(\frac{\hat{p}_{\text{always}}}{\hat{p}_{\text{rarely/never}}}\right) = \hat\beta_{0,A} + \hat\beta_{1,A}x_1 + \cdots + \hat\beta_{p,A}x_p\]

Each equation has its own set of coefficients, and predicted probabilities for all three classes sum to 1.

5.2 Predictors Selected

Predictor Type Justification
ppage Numeric Age is a well-established predictor of voting frequency
educ Categorical Education strongly associated with civic participation
race Categorical Demographic differences in voting behaviour are well-documented
gender Categorical Gender differences in political engagement
income_cat Categorical Higher income linked to higher voter turnout
party Categorical (derived) Party affiliation is one of the strongest predictors of voting consistency

6 Data Splitting

The data is split 80% training / 20% test, stratified by voter_category to ensure all three classes are proportionally represented in both sets.

set.seed(42)
nonvoters_split <- initial_split(nonvoters, prop = 0.80, strata = voter_category)
nonvoters_train <- training(nonvoters_split)
nonvoters_test  <- testing(nonvoters_split)

cat("Training observations:", nrow(nonvoters_train), "\n")
## Training observations: 4667
cat("Test observations:    ", nrow(nonvoters_test),  "\n")
## Test observations:     1169
# Verify stratification held
nonvoters_train %>%
  count(voter_category) %>%
  mutate(pct = round(n / sum(n) * 100, 1)) %>%
  knitr::kable(caption = "Training Set: Voter Category Distribution")
Training Set: Voter Category Distribution
voter_category n pct
rarely/never 1160 24.9
sporadic 2059 44.1
always 1448 31.0

7 Model Fitting

multi_spec <- multinom_reg() %>%
  set_engine("nnet") %>%
  set_mode("classification")

final_model <- multi_spec %>%
  fit(voter_category ~ ppage + educ + race + gender + income_cat + party,
      data = nonvoters_train)

# Repair call so augment works correctly on full data
final_model <- repair_call(final_model, data = nonvoters_train)

7.1 Full Coefficient Table

tidy(final_model) %>%
  mutate_if(is.numeric, ~round(., 4)) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    caption = "Multinomial Logistic Regression Coefficients",
    col.names = c("Outcome", "Term", "Estimate", "Std. Error", "Statistic", "p-value")
  )
Multinomial Logistic Regression Coefficients
Outcome Term Estimate Std. Error Statistic p-value
sporadic (Intercept) -1.5660 0.2084 -7.5127 0.0000
sporadic ppage 0.0470 0.0026 17.9844 0.0000
sporadic educHigh school or less -0.8933 0.1092 -8.1796 0.0000
sporadic educSome college -0.2621 0.1065 -2.4613 0.0138
sporadic raceHispanic 0.0492 0.1420 0.3463 0.7291
sporadic raceOther/Mixed -0.1230 0.1810 -0.6793 0.4969
sporadic raceWhite -0.0381 0.1197 -0.3181 0.7504
sporadic genderMale -0.1453 0.0810 -1.7937 0.0729
sporadic income_cat$40-75k -0.0766 0.1256 -0.6099 0.5419
sporadic income_cat$75-125k -0.0693 0.1193 -0.5808 0.5613
sporadic income_catLess than $40k -0.5824 0.1282 -4.5434 0.0000
sporadic partyIndependent 0.5435 0.1233 4.4076 0.0000
sporadic partyDemocrat 0.8959 0.1174 7.6325 0.0000
sporadic partyRepublican 0.7586 0.1247 6.0849 0.0000
always (Intercept) -2.8582 0.2410 -11.8614 0.0000
always ppage 0.0593 0.0029 20.5945 0.0000
always educHigh school or less -1.2619 0.1220 -10.3410 0.0000
always educSome college -0.2958 0.1148 -2.5773 0.0100
always raceHispanic -0.4398 0.1663 -2.6438 0.0082
always raceOther/Mixed -0.4552 0.2083 -2.1855 0.0289
always raceWhite 0.0784 0.1312 0.5977 0.5500
always genderMale -0.2654 0.0895 -2.9661 0.0030
always income_cat$40-75k -0.0665 0.1369 -0.4859 0.6270
always income_cat$75-125k 0.0828 0.1278 0.6478 0.5171
always income_catLess than $40k -0.6860 0.1431 -4.7928 0.0000
always partyIndependent 0.8638 0.1523 5.6715 0.0000
always partyDemocrat 1.3934 0.1456 9.5719 0.0000
always partyRepublican 1.2203 0.1514 8.0624 0.0000

8 Model Equations

The fitted model produces two log-odds equations relative to the "rarely/never" baseline. Coefficients are taken directly from the model output above.

8.1 Sporadic vs Rarely/Never

\[ \log\left(\frac{\hat{p}_{\text{sporadic}}}{\hat{p}_{\text{rarely/never}}}\right) = -1.566 + 0.0470 \times \text{Age} - 0.893 \times \text{Educ: HS or less} - 0.262 \times \text{Educ: Some college} \] \[ + 0.049 \times \text{Race: Hispanic} - 0.123 \times \text{Race: Other/Mixed} - 0.038 \times \text{Race: White} - 0.145 \times \text{Gender: Male} \] \[ - 0.077 \times \text{Income: \$40-75k} - 0.069 \times \text{Income: \$75-125k} - 0.582 \times \text{Income: <\$40k} + 0.544 \times \text{Party: Independent} + 0.896 \times \text{Party: Democrat} + 0.759 \times \text{Party: Republican} \]

8.2 Always vs Rarely/Never

\[ \log\left(\frac{\hat{p}_{\text{always}}}{\hat{p}_{\text{rarely/never}}}\right) = -2.858 + 0.0593 \times \text{Age} - 1.262 \times \text{Educ: HS or less} - 0.296 \times \text{Educ: Some college} \] \[ - 0.440 \times \text{Race: Hispanic} - 0.455 \times \text{Race: Other/Mixed} + 0.078 \times \text{Race: White} - 0.265 \times \text{Gender: Male} \] \[ - 0.067 \times \text{Income: \$40-75k} + 0.083 \times \text{Income: \$75-125k} - 0.686 \times \text{Income: <\$40k} + 0.864 \times \text{Party: Independent} + 1.393 \times \text{Party: Democrat} + 1.220 \times \text{Party: Republican} \]

9 Coefficient Interpretation

We focus on key predictors from the Always vs Rarely/Never equation, interpreting both in log-odds and odds scale.

Always vs Rarely/Never: Coefficients and Odds Ratios
Predictor Log-Odds Estimate Odds Ratio (exp(β))
ppage 0.0593 1.061
educHigh school or less -1.2619 0.283
educSome college -0.2958 0.744
raceHispanic -0.4398 0.644
raceOther/Mixed -0.4552 0.634
raceWhite 0.0784 1.082
genderMale -0.2654 0.767
income_cat$40-75k -0.0665 0.936
income_cat$75-125k 0.0828 1.086
income_catLess than $40k -0.6860 0.504
partyIndependent 0.8638 2.372
partyDemocrat 1.3934 4.029
partyRepublican 1.2203 3.388

Key interpretations (Always vs Rarely/Never):

  • Age (ppage): Each additional year of age increases the log-odds of being an always voter vs rarely/never. In odds terms, each year multiplies the odds of being a consistent voter by approximately 1.061.

  • Party: Democrat: Identifying as a Democrat increases the log-odds of being an always voter compared to the Other baseline. Democrats have approximately 4.03 times the odds of being a consistent voter relative to those with no/other party affiliation.

  • Party: Republican: Republicans have approximately 3.39 times the odds of being an always voter compared to the Other baseline.

  • Party: Independent: Independents have approximately 2.37 times the odds of always voting compared to the Other group.

  • Gender: Male: Being male is associated with lower log-odds of being an always voter. Males have approximately 0.77 times the odds of being a consistent voter relative to females.

  • Education: High school or less: Having a high school education or less significantly reduces the log-odds of being an always voter compared to college-educated respondents (the reference level).

10 Model Evaluation

10.1 Test Set Predictions

test_aug <- augment(final_model, new_data = nonvoters_test)

test_aug %>%
  select(voter_category, .pred_class,
         `.pred_rarely/never`, `.pred_sporadic`, `.pred_always`) %>%
  head(10) %>%
  mutate_if(is.numeric, ~round(., 3)) %>%
  knitr::kable(caption = "Sample Predicted Probabilities (Test Set, first 10 rows)")
Sample Predicted Probabilities (Test Set, first 10 rows)
voter_category .pred_class .pred_rarely/never .pred_sporadic .pred_always
sporadic sporadic 0.095 0.485 0.421
always sporadic 0.064 0.507 0.429
always always 0.042 0.475 0.483
always always 0.059 0.469 0.472
always sporadic 0.107 0.502 0.391
always sporadic 0.135 0.494 0.371
always always 0.050 0.411 0.539
always sporadic 0.154 0.513 0.333
sporadic always 0.046 0.437 0.516
sporadic sporadic 0.271 0.472 0.257

10.2 Confusion Matrix

conf_mat <- test_aug %>%
  count(voter_category, .pred_class, .drop = FALSE)

conf_mat %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = .pred_class, values_from = n) %>%
  knitr::kable(caption = "Confusion Matrix: Actual (rows) vs Predicted (columns)")
Confusion Matrix: Actual (rows) vs Predicted (columns)
voter_category rarely/never sporadic always
rarely/never 117 164 10
sporadic 52 385 78
always 40 237 86 
conf_mat %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = voter_category, y = n, fill = .pred_class)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", position = "fill") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "rarely/never" = "#F44336",
    "sporadic"     = "#FF9800",
    "always"       = "#4CAF50"
  )) +
  labs(
    title    = "Predicted vs Actual Voter Category (Test Set)",
    subtitle = "Colour shows predicted class; x-axis shows actual class",
    x = "Actual Voter Category",
    y = "Proportion",
    fill = "Predicted Class"
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 11)

10.3 Classification Accuracy

accuracy_val <- test_aug %>%
  accuracy(truth = voter_category, estimate = .pred_class)

test_aug %>%
  metrics(truth = voter_category, estimate = .pred_class) %>%
  mutate_if(is.numeric, ~round(., 4)) %>%
  knitr::kable(caption = "Test Set Classification Metrics")
Test Set Classification Metrics
.metric .estimator .estimate
accuracy multiclass 0.5030
kap multiclass 0.1893

10.4 Deviance Residuals

Deviance residuals allow us to assess model fit at the individual level for each outcome class.

pred_probs <- predict(final_model$fit,
                      newdata = nonvoters_test,
                      type = "probs")

y_actual <- model.matrix(~ voter_category - 1, data = nonvoters_test)

deviance_resid <- sign(y_actual - pred_probs) * sqrt(
  2 * (y_actual * log(ifelse(y_actual == 0, 1, y_actual / pred_probs)) +
       (1 - y_actual) * log(ifelse(y_actual == 0, 1,
                                   (1 - y_actual) / (1 - pred_probs))))
)

resid_df <- cbind(nonvoters_test, deviance_resid) %>%
  as_tibble()

colnames(resid_df)[(ncol(resid_df) - 2):ncol(resid_df)] <-
  c("resid_rarely_never", "resid_sporadic", "resid_always")

p_r <- ggplot(resid_df,
              aes(x = pred_probs[, "rarely/never"], y = resid_rarely_never)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3, color = "#F44336") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "black", linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(title = "Rarely/Never", x = "Fitted Probability", y = "Deviance Residual") +
  theme_bw(base_size = 10)

p_s <- ggplot(resid_df,
              aes(x = pred_probs[, "sporadic"], y = resid_sporadic)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3, color = "#FF9800") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "black", linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(title = "Sporadic", x = "Fitted Probability", y = "Deviance Residual") +
  theme_bw(base_size = 10)

p_a <- ggplot(resid_df,
              aes(x = pred_probs[, "always"], y = resid_always)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3, color = "#4CAF50") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "black", linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(title = "Always", x = "Fitted Probability", y = "Deviance Residual") +
  theme_bw(base_size = 10)

gridExtra::grid.arrange(p_r, p_s, p_a, ncol = 3,
  top = "Deviance Residuals vs Fitted Probability by Voter Class (Test Set)")

Diagnostic interpretation:

  • Rarely/Never and Sporadic: Residuals are tightly clustered near zero across the range of fitted probabilities, indicating good calibration for these two classes.
  • Always: Residuals are more scattered and skewed above zero, suggesting the model underestimates the probability of being an always voter for some respondents. This pattern is consistent with the confusion matrix showing more misclassification in this class.

11 Summary

11.1 Final Model

The final multinomial logistic regression model predicts voter category using age, education, race, gender, income, and party identification. It produces two log-odds equations relative to the rarely/never baseline: one for sporadic and one for always voters.

11.2 Performance Summary

Final Model: Summary Statistics
Metric Value
Response Variable voter_category (3 classes)
Baseline Category rarely/never
Predictors Age, Education, Race, Gender, Income, Party
Training Observations 4667
Test Observations 1169
Test Accuracy 50.3%
Strongest Predictor Party identification (Democrat/Republican)
Model Engine nnet::multinom via tidymodels

11.3 Key Conclusions

  • Party identification is the dominant predictor. Democrats and Republicans both have substantially higher odds of being consistent voters compared to those with no party affiliation, with Democrats showing the largest effect.

  • Age consistently increases voting frequency. Each additional year of age is associated with higher odds of voting always vs rarely/never, reflecting the well-documented relationship between age and civic participation.

  • Education matters significantly. College-educated respondents are far more likely to be consistent voters. Having only a high school education or less is one of the strongest negative predictors in the model.

  • Gender has a modest but consistent effect. Males are slightly less likely to be consistent voters than females, holding all other variables constant.

  • The model generalises reasonably to unseen data. The test accuracy of 50.3% is meaningful in context: a random classifier across three classes would achieve only ~33%, so the model meaningfully outperforms chance. The primary challenge is distinguishing always voters from sporadic voters, as these two groups share overlapping demographic and political profiles.
